q: Heat transfer is a discipline of thermal engineering that concerns the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal energy between physical systems.
The presence of a temperature difference is one of the basic requirements for heat transfer.
Net heat transfer can occur between two systems that are at the same temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by conduction process.
The individual parts of a medium move as a whole during the process of heat conduction.
In heat conduction, heat energy is transferred by successive distribution among the adjacent parts.
Heat energy transferred internally via conduction involves the vibrations of atoms and molecules.
Metals are generally poor conductors of heat because they have very few free electrons.
The thermal conductivity of a material is a measure of its ability to conduct heat.
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called the thermal resistance gradient.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
A negative sign in the differential form of Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must increase as distance $x$ increases.
The SI unit for heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($W \cdot m^{-2}$).
A low value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal conductor.
Materials that are good electric conductors, such as copper and silver, are also good heat conductors.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by movement of the fluid itself.
In heat convection, the transfer of energy occurs due to fluid motion.
Convection currents are set up in a fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is denser than the cooler part.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a solid.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
Newton's law of cooling is the basic equation for the rate of radiation heat transfer.
When $T_2 < T_1$ in the convection equation, heat is lost by the object and $H_{conv}$ is negative.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that requires a dense material medium to propagate.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest mode of heat transfer, traveling at the speed of light.
Energy transfer by radiation suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
Thermal radiation is identical to electromagnetic radiation such as X-rays and gamma rays in its relationship to temperature.
A black object is a poor absorber and a poor radiator of thermal energy.
The emissivity of a black object is close to 1.
A white or silver coated object is a poor absorber and a poor radiator whose emissivity is much less than 1.
A shiny object may reflect a great deal of visible light but still be a good absorber of infrared light.
Stefan-Boltzmann law states that the rate of heat radiation is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature.
A blackbody is an object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it.
Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of heat and work.
Systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
There is a net heat transfer between two systems that are in thermal equilibrium.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics is a simple definition of thermodynamic equilibrium.
The first law of thermodynamics is an application of the conservation of momentum.
The change in internal energy $\Delta U$ is equal to the net heat flow $Q$ minus the total work $W$ done by the system.
In the formula $\Delta U = Q - W$, $Q$ is negative if heat is added to the system.
Internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on temperature.
Internal energy of an ideal gas is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature.
Neither heat nor work are stored as such in a system.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely identical to either heat or work.
A quasi-static process happens fast enough for the system to change its temperature rapidly.
An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure.
The work done by the system in an isobaric process is simply the pressure multiplied by the change in volume.
An isochoric process occurs at constant volume.
In an isochoric process, no work is done by the system because the volume is constant.
According to the first law, for an isochoric process, the change in internal energy must equal the net work done.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature.
For an ideal gas in an isothermal process, the internal energy remains constant.
In an isothermal process, the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ is equal to the work done.
An adiabatic process involves significant heat transfer into or out of the system.
An adiabatic process can be done by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator.
In an adiabatic process, the first law of thermodynamics is reduced to $\Delta U = -W$.
A heat engine is a system that converts mechanical energy into heat.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a cycle.
Thermal efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of work output to heat input.
Applying the first law to one complete cycle, the net change of internal energy $\Delta U$ is zero.
Gasoline engines and diesel engines are two types of external combustion engines.
In a gasoline engine, the cycle involves the performance of six processes.
During the intake stroke of a gasoline engine, only air is drawn into the cylinder.
During the compression stroke of a gasoline engine, both pressure and temperature rise considerably.
In the explosion stage of a gasoline engine, the volume remains unchanged while pressure increases.
The power stroke is when the hot combustion products expand and push the piston out.
The diesel engine induces a mixture of gasoline vapour and air on its intake stroke.
Diesel fuel ignites immediately upon being sprayed into the cylinder because of the heat and pressure inside.
A Carnot engine is a hypothetical engine that operates on an ideal reversible cycle.
All real engines are more efficient than the Carnot engine due to friction.
A reversible process is one where the system can be returned to its initial condition along the same path.
A process that is carried out very quickly can be considered a reversible process in thermodynamics.
The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes and two adiabatic processes.
Step 1 of the Carnot cycle is the reversible adiabatic expansion of the gas.
During isothermal expansion in a Carnot engine, the gas expansion is propelled by absorption of heat from the high temperature reservoir.
Step 2 of the Carnot cycle involves the gas expanding and doing work on the surroundings while being thermally insulated.
Step 3 of the Carnot cycle is reversible isothermal compression where heat is rejected to a low reservoir.
The Carnot engine is $100 \%$ efficient in transforming heat into work.
Carnot's theorem states that all reversible engines operating between the same heat reservoirs are equally efficient.
